$schema: see scripts/_extract_glossary_candidates.py for how candidates were seeded
_format: Each entry: term (canonical name), aliases (alt spellings/abbreviations for search), category (used for grouping/badges), certs (which exams cover it), definition (short, AWS-official-style)

entries.definition: A fully managed service for creating, publishing, maintaining, monitoring, and securing REST, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs at any scale. Supports request validation, transformation via mapping templates, custom and Lambda authorizers, throttling, usage plans, and API keys.

A fully managed integration service for securely transferring data between SaaS applications (Salesforce, ServiceNow, Slack, etc.) and AWS services like Amazon S3 and Amazon Redshift, without writing custom code.
A fully managed non-persistent application and desktop streaming service that delivers desktop applications to any computer through a web browser.
An interactive serverless query service that makes it easy to analyse data directly in Amazon S3 using standard SQL. No infrastructure to manage; you pay only for the queries that you run.

A MySQL- and PostgreSQL-compatible relational database built for the cloud, providing up to 5x throughput of standard MySQL and 3x of PostgreSQL. Storage auto-scales up to 128 TB; supports Aurora Serverless and Global Database.

A fully managed service that offers a choice of high-performing foundation models from leading AI companies through a single API, with capabilities to build generative-AI applications using your own data via retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).
A communications service that lets users meet, chat, and place business calls inside and outside your organization, all using a single application.

A fast content delivery network (CDN) that securely delivers data, videos, applications, and APIs to customers globally with low latency. Integrates with AWS Shield, WAF, ACM, and S3; supports signed URLs/cookies, field-level encryption, and Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions.
A managed search service that lets you set up, manage, and scale a search solution for your website or application without operating search infrastructure.

A monitoring and observability service that collects metrics, logs, and events from AWS resources, applications, and on-premises servers. Includes CloudWatch Alarms, Dashboards, Logs Insights, Synthetics, and Contributor Insights.

An identity service that handles user sign-up, sign-in, and access control. Cognito User Pools provide a directory and authentication for application users; Identity Pools grant temporary AWS credentials to authenticated and guest users.

A natural-language processing (NLP) service that uses machine learning to extract insights and relationships from unstructured text, including entity recognition, sentiment, key phrases, topics, and language detection.
An easy-to-use omnichannel cloud contact centre that helps companies deliver superior customer service at lower cost. Pay-by-the-minute pricing with no infrastructure to manage.

A fully managed, MongoDB-compatible document database service that supports JSON workloads with millisecond latency at any scale. Decouples storage and compute; replicates six copies of data across three Availability Zones.

A fully managed serverless NoSQL key-value and document database that delivers single-digit-millisecond performance at any scale. Supports on-demand and provisioned capacity, global tables, DAX caching, point-in-time recovery, and DynamoDB Streams for change-data capture.
Block-storage volumes for use with Amazon EC2 instances. Volume types (gp3, gp2, io2, io1, st1, sc1) trade off performance and cost; snapshots are stored in S3. Volumes are Availability Zone-scoped.

Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud provides resizable compute capacity in the cloud as virtual machines called instances. Purchasing options include On-Demand, Reserved Instances, Savings Plans, and Spot Instances; instance types span general purpose, compute, memory, storage, and accelerated computing families.

A fully managed container image registry that integrates with Amazon ECS, EKS, Lambda, and Docker CLI. Supports private and public repositories, image scanning, lifecycle policies, and replication across Regions.

A highly scalable container orchestration service that supports Docker containers and lets you run and scale containerized applications on AWS. Two launch types: EC2 (manage your own instances) or Fargate (serverless).

A fully managed, elastic, serverless NFS file system that grows and shrinks automatically. Mountable from multiple EC2 instances, ECS tasks, Lambda functions, and on-premises servers; supports lifecycle management to Infrequent Access and Archive tiers.
A managed Kubernetes service that runs upstream Kubernetes on AWS without you needing to install, operate, and maintain your own Kubernetes control plane. Supports EC2 and Fargate worker nodes.

A fully managed in-memory caching service supporting Redis and Memcached engines. Used to improve application response times and reduce load on backend databases via lazy loading, write-through, or session caching patterns.
A cloud big-data platform for running large-scale distributed data processing jobs using open-source frameworks such as Apache Spark, Hive, Presto, HBase, and Hadoop.

A serverless event bus service that makes it easy to connect application data from your own apps, SaaS, and AWS services via event rules. Supports schema discovery, archive and replay, and scheduled events (successor to CloudWatch Events).

Fully managed third-party file systems on AWS: Windows File Server (SMB), Lustre (HPC), NetApp ONTAP, and OpenZFS. Choose the file system that meets your application's compatibility, performance, and management requirements.

A continuous threat-detection service that monitors AWS accounts and workloads for malicious activity using machine learning and integrated threat intelligence. Analyses VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, and CloudTrail events.
An automated vulnerability-management service that continually scans EC2 instances, container images in ECR, and Lambda functions for software vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure.

A family of services for ingesting and processing streaming data: Kinesis Data Streams (real-time custom processing with shards), Data Firehose (load streams into S3/Redshift/OpenSearch with no code), and Data Analytics (SQL over streams).
A service for building conversational interfaces (chatbots) using voice and text, powered by the same deep-learning technologies that power Amazon Alexa.
An easy-to-use cloud platform that bundles a virtual server, storage, and networking for a fixed monthly price. Designed for simple workloads such as websites, small apps, and dev/test.
A fully managed data security service that uses machine learning and pattern matching to discover, monitor, and help protect sensitive data (PII, financial data) in Amazon S3.

A managed message broker service for Apache ActiveMQ and RabbitMQ, making it easy to migrate existing applications that rely on industry-standard messaging APIs and protocols (JMS, AMQP, STOMP, MQTT, OpenWire).
A fully managed Apache Kafka service for running production workloads. Removes the operational overhead of cluster management, broker patching, and ZooKeeper coordination.

A fast, reliable, fully managed graph database service supporting both property-graph (Gremlin, openCypher) and RDF (SPARQL) query languages. Ideal for fraud detection, knowledge graphs, and recommendation engines.
A managed service for deploying, operating, and scaling OpenSearch and Elasticsearch clusters in the cloud. Includes built-in Kibana/OpenSearch Dashboards for visualisation.
A service that turns text into lifelike speech using deep learning. Supports dozens of voices across many languages.
A cloud-native, serverless business intelligence service that makes it easy to create and publish interactive dashboards. Pay-per-session pricing; ML-powered insights and natural-language Q&A.
A managed relational database service supporting six engines: Aurora, MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, and SQL Server. Automates provisioning, patching, backups, snapshots, and Multi-AZ failover.

A fully managed petabyte-scale cloud data warehouse. Columnar storage, massively parallel processing, and Redshift Spectrum (query S3 directly). Serverless option provisions and scales capacity automatically.

A service that makes it easy to add image and video analysis to applications: object/scene/activity detection, facial analysis, face comparison, text in images, content moderation, and celebrity recognition.

A highly available and scalable cloud DNS web service. Supports public and private hosted zones, health checks, traffic-routing policies (simple, weighted, latency-based, failover, geolocation, geoproximity, multi-value), and domain registration.

Amazon Simple Storage Service offers industry-leading scalability, data availability, security, and performance for object storage. Storage classes: Standard, Intelligent-Tiering, Standard-IA, One Zone-IA, Glacier Instant/Flexible Retrieval, and Glacier Deep Archive. Features include versioning, lifecycle policies, cross-region replication, pre-signed URLs, and event notifications.

A fully managed service that provides every developer and data scientist with the ability to build, train, and deploy machine learning models at any scale. Includes built-in algorithms, hyperparameter tuning, and model hosting.
A scalable, cost-effective email-sending service for transactional and marketing emails. Supports IP/domain reputation management, DKIM signing, and inbound email processing.

A fully managed pub/sub messaging service. Producers publish to topics; subscribers (SQS queues, Lambda, HTTP/S endpoints, email, SMS, mobile push) receive messages. Supports message filtering and FIFO topics with strict ordering and deduplication.

A fully managed message queuing service that decouples and scales microservices, distributed systems, and serverless applications. Two queue types: Standard (best-effort ordering, at-least-once delivery, near-unlimited throughput) and FIFO (strict ordering, exactly-once processing).
A machine learning service that automatically extracts text, handwriting, forms, and tabular data from scanned documents.
An automatic speech-recognition service that converts audio to text. Supports real-time streaming and batch transcription across many languages.
A neural machine-translation service that delivers fast, high-quality, customisable language translation.

A logically isolated section of the AWS Cloud where you launch AWS resources in a virtual network you define. Configure IP ranges (CIDR), subnets (public/private), route tables, internet/NAT gateways, security groups, and network ACLs.
A managed, secure desktop-as-a-service (DaaS) solution that delivers persistent Windows or Linux desktops to users on demand.

An identifier issued by Amazon API Gateway used to track and control how the API is used by individual clients. Typically combined with usage plans for throttling and quotas. Not a substitute for authentication.

Amazon Resource Names uniquely identify AWS resources across the account, partition, region, and service. Format: arn:partition:service:region:account-id:resource. Used in IAM policies, cross-service references, and CLI/SDK arguments.

One or more discrete data centres with redundant power, networking, and connectivity within an AWS Region. AZs in a Region are physically separated and connected with high-bandwidth, low-latency links. Multi-AZ deployments improve fault tolerance.
A set of tools and services for building scalable mobile and web applications. Includes hosting (CDN-backed), authentication (Cognito), data (DataStore/AppSync), and CI/CD.
A fully managed service that makes it easy to deploy from source code or a container image directly to a scalable, secure web application running on AWS.

A capability of AWS Systems Manager for creating, managing, and deploying application configurations and feature flags. Supports controlled rollouts, validation, and automatic rollback on deployment errors.
A managed serverless GraphQL service that simplifies application development by letting you create flexible APIs to securely access, manipulate, and combine data from multiple sources.
A central resource for compliance reports (SOC, PCI, ISO, FedRAMP, HIPAA, etc.) and security and compliance agreements (AWS BAA) with AWS.
A fully managed service that centralises and automates data protection across AWS services (EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, EFS, FSx, Storage Gateway, EC2 AMIs, S3, and more) and on-premises VMs.

A service that lets developers, scientists, and engineers easily and efficiently run hundreds of thousands of batch computing jobs on AWS. Dynamically provisions optimal compute resources based on the volume and resource requirements of submitted jobs.

Lets you set custom budgets that alert you when your costs or usage exceed (or are forecasted to exceed) your budgeted amount. Supports cost, usage, RI utilisation, and Savings Plans coverage budgets.
An open-source software development framework for defining cloud infrastructure in code (TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Java, Go, C#) and provisioning it through AWS CloudFormation.

A service that lets you easily provision, manage, and deploy public and private SSL/TLS certificates for use with AWS services and your internal connected resources. Public certs are free; auto-renewed.
Guidance and best practices, organised into six perspectives (Business, People, Governance, Platform, Security, Operations), for accelerating an organisation's cloud journey.
A cloud-based integrated development environment (IDE) that lets you write, run, and debug code with just a browser. Pre-packaged with the AWS CLI and common runtimes.

An infrastructure-as-code service that provisions and manages AWS and third-party resources by treating infrastructure as JSON or YAML templates. Supports stacks, change sets, drift detection, nested stacks, StackSets, and DeletionPolicy/UpdateReplacePolicy.
Cloud-based hardware security modules (HSMs) that let you generate and use your own encryption keys in dedicated, FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated hardware.
A browser-based shell pre-authenticated with your AWS Management Console credentials. Comes pre-installed with AWS CLI, common shells (bash, PowerShell, zsh), and runtimes.
A service that enables governance, compliance, and operational/risk auditing of an AWS account. Logs API calls across services into trails delivered to S3, CloudWatch Logs, and EventBridge.
A fully managed software artifact repository that supports popular package managers (npm, PyPI, Maven, NuGet, etc.) and integrates with public package repositories via upstream connections.
A fully managed continuous-integration service that compiles source code, runs tests, and produces software packages ready to deploy. Build instructions live in a buildspec.yml file.
A fully managed source-control service that hosts secure private Git repositories. Integrates with IAM for access control and triggers (CloudWatch Events / Lambda) on repository events.
A deployment service that automates application deployments to EC2, on-premises instances, Lambda, or ECS. Supports in-place, blue/green, canary, and linear deployments via an appspec.yml file.

A continuous-delivery service that automates release pipelines for fast and reliable application and infrastructure updates. Models source -> build -> test -> deploy stages with manual approval actions and parallel execution.

A service that continuously monitors and records AWS resource configurations and lets you automate the evaluation of recorded configurations against desired configurations (Config Rules + auto-remediation).

The easiest way to set up and govern a secure, multi-account AWS environment (a landing zone). Provides guardrails (preventive SCPs + detective AWS Config rules) and account-vending via Service Catalog.
A free tool for viewing and analysing AWS costs and usage over time. Includes filters, grouping, forecasts, RI/Savings Plans recommendations, and Rightsizing Recommendations.

A dedicated, private network connection from on-premises to AWS. Provides consistent network performance, lower bandwidth costs at scale, and the ability to extend on-premises networks into AWS through VLANs.

An easy-to-use service for deploying and scaling web applications and services developed with Java, .NET, PHP, Node.js, Python, Ruby, Go, and Docker. You retain full control of the underlying AWS resources; configure via .ebextensions.
A serverless compute engine for containers that works with Amazon ECS and EKS. You don't manage EC2 instances; pay only for the CPU and memory your tasks/pods consume.

A security management service that lets you centrally configure and manage firewall rules (WAF, Shield Advanced, Network Firewall, security groups, Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall) across multiple accounts and resources.
A networking service that improves the availability and performance of applications by routing traffic through the AWS global network and providing static anycast IP addresses as a fixed entry point.

Provides a personalised view of the performance and availability of AWS services that you are using, plus alerts about scheduled changes and outages that affect your resources (Personal Health) alongside global service health (Service Health).

A service for securely controlling access to AWS resources. Manages users, groups, roles, identity providers, and policies (identity-based, resource-based, permission boundaries, SCPs). Apply the principle of least privilege and protect the root user with MFA.

Successor to AWS Single Sign-On. Centrally manages workforce access to multiple AWS accounts and business applications. Supports SAML 2.0 IdPs and built-in identity store; assigns permission sets to users and groups.

A managed service for creating and controlling cryptographic keys used to encrypt data. Supports customer-managed keys (CMKs), AWS-managed keys, key rotation, key policies, grants, and envelope encryption via data keys.

A serverless compute service that runs code in response to events (API Gateway, S3, DynamoDB Streams, EventBridge, SQS, etc.) without provisioning or managing servers. Pay only for compute time consumed; supports up to 10 GB of memory, 15-min timeout, layers, aliases/versions, and provisioned concurrency.
A service that streamlines managing software licenses (Microsoft, SAP, Oracle, IBM, etc.) from vendors across AWS and on-premises environments.
A curated digital catalog that customers can use to find, buy, deploy, and manage third-party software, data, and services. Charges roll up into the customer's AWS bill.
A single location to discover, plan, and track migrations across multiple AWS and partner solutions.
A managed network firewall service for VPCs. Provides stateful inspection, intrusion prevention (Suricata-compatible rules), and protocol filtering.
A service for centrally managing and governing multiple AWS accounts. Provides consolidated billing, organizational units (OUs), Service Control Policies (SCPs), and resource sharing across accounts.
Fully managed racks (or smaller 1U/2U servers) of AWS-designed infrastructure that bring AWS services and operating model to virtually any on-premises or edge location.
A web-based planning tool you can use to create cost estimates for AWS services. Supports modelling multi-service architectures and exporting estimates as CSV/PDF.
A service that helps you securely share AWS resources (Transit Gateways, subnets, License Manager configurations, Route 53 Resolver rules, etc.) with any AWS account or through AWS Organizations.
A service to manage, retrieve, and rotate database credentials, API keys, and other secrets throughout their lifecycle. Native rotation for RDS, Redshift, DocumentDB; custom rotation via Lambda.

A cloud security posture management (CSPM) service that aggregates, organises, and prioritises security findings from AWS services (GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer) and third-party tools against industry standards (CIS, PCI DSS, AWS Foundational Security Best Practices).

An open-source framework for building serverless applications. SAM templates extend CloudFormation with shorthand syntax for Lambda functions, APIs, and DynamoDB tables. The SAM CLI provides local testing (sam local invoke), guided deploy, and packaging.

Lets organisations create and manage catalogs of IT services (built as CloudFormation products) that are approved for use on AWS. Users browse and self-provision products under governed configurations.

A managed Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) protection service. Shield Standard (free, automatically on for all customers) defends against common network/transport attacks; Shield Advanced (paid) adds proactive engagement with the AWS DDoS Response Team and cost-protection.
Physical devices for petabyte- and exabyte-scale data transfer, edge computing, and storage in environments with limited connectivity. Snowcone (8 TB), Snowball Edge (80 TB), Snowmobile (100 PB).

A serverless workflow service that lets you coordinate multiple AWS services into serverless applications. Define state machines in Amazon States Language (JSON) with Standard (long-running, exactly-once) or Express (high-volume, at-least-once) workflows.
A hybrid cloud storage service that connects on-premises environments to virtually unlimited cloud storage. Gateway types: S3 File, FSx File, Volume (cached/stored), and Tape.

Four tiers: Basic (free, account & billing), Developer (business-hours email, one primary contact), Business (24/7 phone/chat/email, all customers, third-party software guidance), and Enterprise On-Ramp / Enterprise (24/7 with 15-min response for critical issues, designated Technical Account Manager, Infrastructure Event Management, Concierge support team).

An operations hub that provides operational data and automated actions across AWS resources. Includes Parameter Store (config + secrets), Session Manager (shell access without SSH), Patch Manager, Run Command, Inventory, OpsCenter, and Automation.
Fully managed support for file transfers in and out of Amazon S3 and Amazon EFS over SFTP, FTPS, FTP, and AS2 protocols.

An online tool that provides real-time guidance to help you provision resources following AWS best practices across five categories: cost optimisation, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service limits.

A web-application firewall that protects web applications and APIs against common exploits (SQL injection, XSS, bots) by inspecting HTTP/S requests. Deployable on CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, AppSync, and Cognito user pools.

A distributed-tracing service that helps developers analyse and debug production, distributed applications. Visualises requests as a service map; segments and subsegments expose latency and errors per call.

A deployment strategy that runs two identical production environments (blue = current, green = new). Traffic is shifted from blue to green; if issues arise you can instantly roll back by switching traffic back to blue. Native support in CodeDeploy, Elastic Beanstalk, and Lambda alias routing.

A deployment strategy where the new version is rolled out to a small subset of users/instances first. If metrics look good, the rest are migrated. CodeDeploy and API Gateway support canary traffic shifting natively.
Classless Inter-Domain Routing notation for IP address ranges (e.g. 10.0.0.0/16). VPCs and subnets are defined with CIDR blocks; subnet CIDRs must be within and not overlap inside the VPC CIDR.

The latency penalty incurred when AWS Lambda must initialise a new execution environment (download code, start runtime, run init code) before invoking the handler. Mitigated by provisioned concurrency, keeping function packages small, and lazy-loading SDK clients outside the handler.

Amazon DynamoDB Accelerator is a fully managed, write-through, in-memory cache for DynamoDB that delivers microsecond response times for read-heavy workloads. Sits between the app and the DynamoDB table; uses the same DynamoDB API.

Sites that CloudFront uses to cache copies of content for faster delivery to users in any location. There are many more edge locations than Regions, distributed worldwide; also used by Route 53, Global Accelerator, and Shield.

Automatically distributes incoming traffic across multiple targets and Availability Zones. ALB (Layer 7, HTTP/HTTPS, host/path/header rules), NLB (Layer 4, TCP/UDP/TLS, ultra-low latency, static IPs), GWLB (Layer 3 transparent for security appliances), and Classic Load Balancer (legacy).

Protection of data while it is stored on disk. AWS services integrate with KMS for server-side encryption (SSE-KMS, SSE-S3, SSE-C in S3; KMS-encrypted EBS volumes, RDS instances, DynamoDB tables, etc.).

Protection of data while it moves between systems. Implemented with TLS/SSL between clients and AWS endpoints, ACM-managed certificates on load balancers and CloudFront, and VPC peering / VPN / Direct Connect MACsec for inter-network traffic.

A pattern where a customer master key (CMK / KMS key) encrypts a data key, and the data key encrypts the data itself. The encrypted data key is stored alongside the ciphertext; this avoids sending large amounts of data to KMS.

A DynamoDB index with a partition key, optional sort key, and projection that can be different from the base table. Supports eventually consistent reads across all partitions; creatable/deletable at any time.

A property where multiple invocations of the same operation produce the same result. Critical for retry-safe APIs; achieve via deduplication keys (SQS FIFO MessageDeduplicationId, S3 multipart uploads, DynamoDB conditional writes, idempotency tokens in SDK requests).

Practice of managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable definition files rather than manual processes. On AWS: CloudFormation, AWS SAM, AWS CDK, and third-party tools like Terraform/Pulumi.

A horizontally scaled, redundant, highly available VPC component that allows communication between resources in a VPC and the public internet. Required for resources in public subnets to be reachable.
A compact, URL-safe means of representing claims between two parties as a signed JSON object. Commonly issued by Amazon Cognito user pools and consumed by API Gateway authorizers.

A DynamoDB index sharing the same partition key as the base table but with a different sort key. Strongly or eventually consistent reads; must be created at table-creation time and limited to 5 per table.

An extra layer of protection on top of username and password, requiring a second factor (virtual or hardware MFA device, FIDO security key) for sign-in. Strongly recommended for the root user and all privileged IAM identities.

A managed network address translation service that lets resources in a private subnet initiate outbound connections to the internet (or other AWS services) while remaining unreachable from the internet.

A time-limited URL generated by an authorised principal that grants temporary access to an S3 object (GET or PUT) without requiring the requester to have AWS credentials. Common for browser uploads/downloads.
A Lambda feature that initialises a requested number of execution environments and keeps them warm. Eliminates cold starts for predictable workloads. Charged per GB-second of provisioned capacity.
A Lambda configuration that guarantees the maximum number of concurrent executions for a function (and caps it). Prevents one function from consuming all account-level unreserved concurrency.

A physical location in the world where AWS clusters data centres. Each Region consists of multiple, isolated, and physically separate Availability Zones. Customers choose Regions for latency, compliance/data residency, service availability, and cost.

A billing discount applied to EC2 (and a few other services) in exchange for a 1- or 3-year commitment. Up to ~72% off On-Demand prices; Standard (locked attributes) or Convertible (can change instance family). Largely superseded by Savings Plans.

A flexible pricing model offering lower compute prices in exchange for a commitment to a consistent usage amount ($/hour) for 1 or 3 years. Compute Savings Plans (most flexible, EC2/Fargate/Lambda); EC2 Instance Savings Plans (instance-family-specific); SageMaker Savings Plans.

Language-specific libraries that simplify calling AWS APIs from your application. Officially supported SDKs: Python (boto3), JavaScript/TypeScript, Java, Go, .NET, Ruby, PHP, C++, Kotlin, Rust, Swift.

A virtual stateful firewall attached to an EC2 instance / ENI. Allow rules only (no deny rules); return traffic for established connections is automatically permitted. Operates at the instance level (unlike Network ACLs at subnet level).

An AWS Organizations policy type that sets the maximum permissions for accounts in the organisation. SCPs don't grant permissions but act as a guardrail filter over IAM policies. Attached to roots, OUs, or accounts.

AWS's framework for security responsibilities: AWS is responsible for security OF the cloud (hardware, hypervisor, managed services); the customer is responsible for security IN the cloud (data, IAM, OS patching for unmanaged services, application code, network/firewall config).

Spare EC2 capacity at up to 90% off On-Demand prices. AWS can reclaim the instance with a 2-minute notification. Suitable for fault-tolerant, flexible workloads (batch jobs, CI/CD, big data, stateless web).

AWS Security Token Service grants temporary, limited-privilege credentials for IAM principals or federated users via AssumeRole, AssumeRoleWithSAML, AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity, GetSessionToken, and GetFederationToken APIs.
Key-value pairs you assign to AWS resources for metadata, organisation, automation, IAM access control (ABAC), and cost allocation (activate as Cost Allocation Tags in Billing).

A network transit hub that connects VPCs, on-premises networks (via Direct Connect or VPN), and other Transit Gateways in a hub-and-spoke topology. Simplifies what would otherwise be a full mesh of VPC peering connections.

An SQS configuration that hides a message from other consumers after a worker receives it, giving the worker time to process and delete it. If the worker fails to delete before the timeout expires, the message becomes visible again.
AWS Site-to-Site VPN creates encrypted IPsec tunnels between an on-premises network and a VPC. AWS Client VPN provides per-user OpenVPN access to AWS and on-premises networks.

AWS's set of best practices organised into six pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, and Sustainability. The Well-Architected Tool reviews workloads against these pillars.

A template containing the software configuration (OS, application server, applications) required to launch an EC2 instance. AMIs can be owned by AWS, the AWS Marketplace community, or built privately; shared across accounts; copied across Regions.

A service that monitors applications and automatically adjusts capacity to maintain steady, predictable performance at the lowest possible cost. Coordinates scaling across multiple resources (EC2 Auto Scaling groups, ECS services, DynamoDB tables, Aurora replicas) from a single configuration.

Helps maintain application availability by automatically launching or terminating EC2 instances based on policies. Defines an Auto Scaling group with min, max, and desired capacity; scaling policies can be target-tracking, step, simple, predictive, or scheduled.

AWS-designed Arm-based processors that power EC2 instance families (M6g/M7g, C6g/C7g, R6g/R7g, T4g) at up to 40% better price-performance than comparable x86 instances. Supported in Lambda, ECS Fargate, EKS, RDS, and ElastiCache.

A command-line tool that helps developers build, release, and operate production-ready containerized applications on Amazon ECS and AWS Fargate. Generates the underlying CloudFormation templates and CI/CD pipelines from a single `copilot init` workflow.

Analyses configuration and utilisation metrics across EC2 instances, Auto Scaling groups, EBS volumes, Lambda functions, and ECS services on Fargate to recommend right-sized resource configurations that reduce cost and improve performance.
A managed runtime and tooling service to migrate, modernise, run, and operate mainframe applications on AWS. Supports replatform (AWS Blu Age) and refactor (Micro Focus) patterns.

A pointer to a specific Lambda function version. Aliases let you decouple integrations (event sources, API Gateway, callers) from versions and enable weighted alias routing for canary / linear deployments.

An immutable snapshot of a Lambda function's code and configuration. Published versions get a sequential version number; the unpublished latest is referenced as `$LATEST`. Combine with aliases for stable invocation targets.

A .zip archive of libraries, custom runtimes, or other dependencies that you can attach to one or more Lambda functions. Layers reduce deployment-package size, enable sharing of common dependencies, and can be versioned and shared across accounts.

A feature of Amazon CloudFront that runs Lambda functions at AWS edge locations in response to viewer/origin request and response events. Used for HTTP header manipulation, A/B testing, dynamic origin selection, and personalised responses with low latency.

Lightweight JavaScript functions that run at CloudFront edge locations with sub-millisecond startup time. Limited to <1 ms CPU and 2 MB memory; ideal for header/URL rewrites, cache-key normalisation, and simple auth checks. Cheaper and faster than Lambda@Edge for short tasks.

Embeds AWS compute and storage inside 5G networks at the edge of telecom carriers, providing single-digit millisecond latency to mobile devices and end users. Useful for AR/VR, real-time gaming, ML inference, and connected vehicles.

Place compute, storage, database, and other select AWS services close to large population, industry, and IT centres. An extension of an AWS Region; supports applications that require single-digit-millisecond latency to end users.

An EBS volume type (io1, io2, io2 Block Express) where you specify the desired IOPS independently of volume size, up to 256,000 IOPS per volume. Suited for I/O-intensive workloads such as large relational databases and NoSQL stores.

An online data-transfer service that moves data between on-premises storage (NFS, SMB, HDFS, self-managed object storage), edge locations, and AWS storage services (S3, EFS, FSx). Up to 10x faster than open-source tools; preserves metadata and integrity checks.

A petabyte-scale data-transport and edge-compute device in the AWS Snow Family. Storage Optimized variants (up to 80 TB usable) for bulk migration; Compute Optimized variants offer onboard EC2 and GPU for edge processing in disconnected environments.

The smallest member of the AWS Snow Family (8 TB HDD or 14 TB SSD). Portable, rugged, secure, designed for collecting data at the edge in space-constrained or austere environments. Integrates with AWS DataSync for online sync to AWS.

Low-cost S3 storage classes designed for archival data. Glacier Instant Retrieval (ms access, archive infrequent), Glacier Flexible Retrieval (minutes-to-hours retrieval), and Glacier Deep Archive (cheapest, 12-48 hour retrieval) trade retrieval time for storage price.

An S3 storage class that automatically moves objects between Frequent, Infrequent, Archive Instant, Archive, and Deep Archive access tiers based on changing access patterns, with no retrieval fees or operational overhead.
Uses the global Amazon CloudFront edge-location network to accelerate uploads and downloads to S3 buckets. Useful for transferring large objects over long geographic distances; enabled per bucket.

Enables you to keep multiple variants of an object in the same bucket so you can recover from unintended user actions and application failures. Combined with MFA Delete and lifecycle policies for compliance / retention controls.

Automatically copies S3 objects to one or more destination buckets in the same or different Regions. Supports replica modification sync, replication time control (RTC) for 15-minute SLAs, and KMS-key replication.

A write-once-read-many (WORM) feature that prevents objects from being deleted or overwritten for a fixed amount of time or indefinitely. Two retention modes: Governance (with special IAM permission to override) and Compliance (immutable, even by root).
Automates creation, retention, and deletion of EBS snapshots and EBS-backed AMIs according to schedule-based policies. Used for backup, disaster recovery, and AMI hygiene.

A fully managed CI/CD and hosting service for fast, secure, reliable static and server-side rendered web apps (Next.js, Nuxt, etc.). Git-based workflow with preview environments, custom domains, branch deployments, and edge content delivery via CloudFront.

An S3 API that splits a large object (>100 MB recommended, required >5 GB) into parts uploaded in parallel and reassembled on completion. Improves throughput, supports pause/resume, and is required for very large objects.

Asynchronous, automated replication of S3 objects to a destination bucket in a different AWS Region. Useful for compliance, lower-latency access in distant geographies, and operational redundancy. Configured at the bucket level via S3 Replication rules.

A fully managed, highly available database proxy for RDS and Aurora that pools and shares database connections. Reduces application latency under bursty workloads (especially serverless Lambda) and improves failover times.
An on-demand auto-scaling configuration for Amazon Aurora. v2 scales instance capacity in fine-grained increments to meet workload demand; you pay per-second for the database capacity used.
An Aurora deployment that spans multiple AWS Regions for disaster recovery and low-latency global reads. Replicates with typical lag under 1 second; supports cross-Region failover in under a minute.

A time-ordered sequence of item-level changes (inserts, updates, deletes) in a DynamoDB table. Records are stored for 24 hours; consumed by Lambda triggers, Kinesis Data Streams, or the DynamoDB Streams API for replication, materialised views, or notifications.
A multi-Region, multi-active replication of a DynamoDB table. Provides single-digit-millisecond local reads and writes from any participating Region, with last-writer-wins conflict resolution.

DynamoDB APIs that group up to 100 operations across multiple items and tables into all-or-nothing ACID transactions. Useful when several items must be updated atomically (financial transfers, inventory reservations).
A DynamoDB billing mode that automatically scales capacity to accommodate workloads with no minimum throughput. Pay per request; ideal for unpredictable traffic and new applications.

A DynamoDB billing mode where you specify read and write capacity units per table. Combine with auto-scaling to track utilisation targets; cheaper than on-demand when traffic is predictable and steady.
A copy of an RDS / Aurora database used to offload read traffic from the primary. Async replication; can be promoted to a standalone instance for disaster recovery or cross-Region failover.

An RDS / Aurora high-availability configuration that maintains a synchronous standby replica in a different Availability Zone. Automatic failover within 60-120 seconds; transparent to application connections via the RDS endpoint.

Provides private connectivity between VPCs, AWS services, and on-premises networks without exposing traffic to the public internet. Connections use VPC interface endpoints (ENIs) inside your VPC with private IP addresses.

Lets you privately connect a VPC to supported AWS services without internet, NAT, VPN, or Direct Connect. Two types: Gateway endpoints (S3, DynamoDB â€” free) and Interface endpoints (powered by PrivateLink, paid).

A networking connection between two VPCs that lets instances communicate as if they were within the same network. Non-transitive; CIDR blocks must not overlap. Supports cross-Region and cross-account peerings.

A cloud resource discovery service. Lets you define custom names for application resources and maintain the updated location of these dynamic resources. Integrates with App Mesh, ECS, and Route 53 for service discovery in microservice architectures.

A managed client-based VPN service that enables you to securely access AWS resources and resources in your on-premises network. Uses OpenVPN-based client; integrates with IAM, Active Directory, and SAML for authentication.
Creates two encrypted IPsec tunnels between an on-premises customer gateway and an AWS virtual private gateway or transit gateway. Provides a quick, low-cost alternative or backup to Direct Connect.
A stateless firewall that controls traffic in and out of a subnet using ordered allow / deny rules. Complements Security Groups (which are stateful and attached to instances).

A set of rules (routes) that determine where network traffic from a subnet or gateway is directed. Each subnet must be associated with a route table; routes can point to internet gateways, NAT gateways, peering connections, transit gateways, or VPC endpoints.

A virtual network card attached to an EC2 instance or other AWS resource (Lambda VPC config, ECS task, etc.). Has a primary private IPv4, optional secondary IPs, Elastic IP, MAC address, and security groups.
A static public IPv4 address allocated to your AWS account and remappable between EC2 instances or ENIs. Free while associated with a running instance; charged when allocated but unused.

A central console for managing AWS and on-premises networks. Provides a visual representation of your global network, route propagation, traffic, and performance across Transit Gateways, VPN, Direct Connect, and SD-WAN partners.

Automatically collects log data from VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail, and GuardDuty findings, applies machine learning and graph theory to build a linked set of data so analysts can investigate, isolate, and resolve security issues.

Helps continuously audit AWS usage to assess risk and compliance with regulations (PCI DSS, GDPR, HIPAA, AWS Foundational Security Best Practices) and industry standards. Automates evidence collection and produces audit-ready reports.

A client-side encryption library that helps developers implement industry best practices: envelope encryption, key reuse via data-key caching, support for multiple key providers (KMS, custom keystores), and pluggable cryptographic algorithms. Available in Java, Python, C, JavaScript, .NET.

Manages code-signing certificates and verifies signatures for Lambda functions, IoT devices, and container images. Ensures only trusted code is deployed; integrates with KMS-backed certificates from AWS Certificate Manager Private CA.

Identifies resources in your organisation and accounts (S3 buckets, IAM roles, KMS keys, Lambda functions, SQS queues, Secrets Manager secrets) that are shared with an external entity. Continuously monitors policies and surfaces unintended external access.

Provides multiple ways to use Microsoft Active Directory with other AWS services: AWS Managed Microsoft AD (full AD service), AD Connector (proxy to on-prem AD), and Simple AD (Samba 4-based, low-cost).

An advanced IAM feature that uses a managed policy to set the maximum permissions an identity-based policy can grant to a user or role. Useful for delegating permission management to other teams without granting overly broad privileges.

A JSON policy document attached to a resource (S3 bucket, SNS topic, SQS queue, Lambda function, KMS key, etc.) that grants the specified Principal permissions to act on the resource. Pair with identity-based policies for cross-account access.

A JSON policy attached to an IAM identity (user, group, or role) that grants permissions on AWS resources. Managed (AWS-managed, customer-managed) or inline; evaluated together with resource-based policies and SCPs.
An IAM role's resource-based policy that defines which principals (users, services, accounts) can assume the role. Combined with the role's permissions policy to determine effective access.

Paid tier of AWS Shield that provides expanded DDoS protection with the AWS Shield Response Team (SRT) for 24/7 incident response, near-real-time visibility into attacks, cost protection for scaling caused by attacks, and integration with AWS WAF and Firewall Manager.
A 24/7 team of DDoS experts available to Shield Advanced subscribers (and Business/Enterprise Support customers) for assistance with DDoS incident triage, mitigation, and post-event analysis.

AWS's process for authenticating requests by signing them with your access key. Most AWS SDKs sign requests automatically; required by all current AWS service APIs. Includes a date/region/service scope and a hash of the request body.

A unified tool to manage AWS services from a command shell. Supports scripting and automation across services; uses named profiles for credentials; v2 adds SSO support, auto-prompt mode, and parameter validation.

The browser-based graphical interface to AWS services. Lets administrators and developers provision, configure, monitor, and manage resources without using the CLI/SDK. Mobile companion app available.

IDE extensions for VS Code, JetBrains, Eclipse, and Visual Studio that provide an integrated experience for developing serverless applications on AWS: editing SAM templates, deploying Lambda, viewing CloudWatch logs, and remote debugging.

The command-line tooling for AWS SAM. `sam init` scaffolds projects, `sam build` packages dependencies, `sam local invoke / start-api` runs Lambdas locally, `sam deploy` uploads to CloudFormation, and `sam logs / sync` accelerate the inner-loop.

A YAML file that defines the build commands and settings used by AWS CodeBuild. Phases (install, pre_build, build, post_build, reports, artifacts) describe what to run; environment variables and cache locations are also configured here.

A YAML file that defines deployment instructions for AWS CodeDeploy. Specifies source-to-target file mappings, lifecycle event hooks (BeforeInstall, AfterInstall, ApplicationStart, etc.) and, for ECS or Lambda, traffic-shift parameters.

A generative-AI-powered assistant for software development and AWS use. Amazon Q Developer suggests code, explains APIs, refactors, generates tests, and answers AWS-architecture questions; can chat with private code repositories when enabled.

A unified user interface that lets developers quickly develop, build, and deploy applications on AWS using preconfigured project templates. Aggregates CodeCommit/Build/Deploy/Pipeline and JIRA into a single dashboard.

Two ML-powered services: CodeGuru Reviewer (automated code reviews for Java and Python with security and performance recommendations) and CodeGuru Profiler (production profiling to identify the most expensive lines of code).

A component of Amazon CloudWatch that monitors, stores, and accesses log files from EC2, Lambda, CloudTrail, Route 53, and other sources. Supports retention policies, metric filters that emit CloudWatch metrics, subscription filters to Kinesis/Firehose/Lambda, and log group encryption with KMS.
An interactive query language for searching and analysing log data in CloudWatch Logs. Pay per scanned data; results render in tables or charts that can be saved to dashboards.

Watches a single CloudWatch metric over a specified time period and performs actions (SNS notification, Auto Scaling, EC2 Stop/Reboot/Terminate, Systems Manager OpsCenter incident) when the threshold is breached. Supports composite alarms across multiple metrics.

Configurable scripts (canaries) that monitor endpoints and APIs from outside-in by following customer flows. Detects broken links, slow page load, and outages before customers notice; integrates with CloudWatch alarms.

A capability of AWS Systems Manager that provides secure, hierarchical storage for configuration data and secrets. Plain-text String, StringList, and KMS-encrypted SecureString parameter types; native IAM integration.

A browser-based or CLI shell that gives interactive one-click access to managed EC2 instances and on-prem servers without opening SSH/RDP, bastion hosts, or managing keys. Sessions are logged to CloudWatch Logs / S3 for audit.

Automates the process of patching managed instances with both security and other types of updates. Patch baselines define which patches are approved; maintenance windows schedule when patches are applied.

Lets you create runbooks (Automation documents) to perform common maintenance and deployment tasks across AWS resources. Includes AWS-curated documents and the ability to author custom JSON/YAML automations.

Extends CloudFormation stacks to provision the same template across multiple accounts and Regions in a single operation. Used for organization-wide guardrails, IAM baselines, and multi-account landing-zone deployments.

Detects whether a stack's actual configuration differs from the configuration expected by its CloudFormation template (because someone modified resources outside CloudFormation). Reports per-resource drift status and lets you re-import.

Collects and presents information about your on-premises servers, applications, and network dependencies to plan a migration. Two collectors: agent-based (richer data) or agentless (VMware-only). Data flows into Migration Hub for centralised tracking.

AWS-staffed operations service for enterprises that lets you offload ongoing infrastructure-management tasks. Includes 24/7 incident management, change management, security and patch management, monitoring, and continuity. Two tiers: Accelerate and Advanced.
An offering on top of Enterprise Support that provides proactive monitoring of workloads by AWS engineers. Detects incidents in less than five minutes and engages the customer for rapid mitigation.

A point-to-point integration in EventBridge that connects an event source (SQS, Kinesis, DynamoDB Streams, Kafka, MQ) to a target (Lambda, Step Functions, SNS, API destinations) with optional filtering, batching, and enrichment via Lambda / Step Functions / API destinations.
A serverless scheduler that lets you create, run, and manage tasks at any scale. Supports one-time and recurring schedules with delivery to over 270 AWS services and 6,000+ API operations.

A massively scalable real-time data streaming service. Streams are composed of shards (provisioned mode) or on-demand throughput; producers write records via the Kinesis Producer Library (KPL) and consumers read via the Kinesis Client Library (KCL) or Lambda.

A fully managed service that delivers real-time streaming data to destinations such as S3, Redshift, OpenSearch, Splunk, and HTTP endpoints. Auto-scales with no shards to manage; supports data transformation via Lambda and format conversion to Parquet/ORC.

An SQS queue type guaranteeing exactly-once processing and strict ordering of messages within a message group. Up to 300 transactions per second (TPS) without batching, 3,000 TPS with batching, or higher-throughput mode for higher TPS.

The default SQS queue type with at-least-once delivery, best-effort ordering, and nearly unlimited throughput. Use for high-throughput workloads that can tolerate occasional duplicates and out-of-order messages.

A secondary SQS queue (or SNS / Lambda destination) that receives messages that couldn't be processed successfully after a maximum number of attempts. Used to isolate problematic messages for debugging without blocking the main workflow.

An SQS consumer technique that waits up to 20 seconds for messages to arrive before returning empty, reducing the number of empty receives and API calls. Set via the ReceiveMessageWaitTimeSeconds queue attribute or per-call WaitTimeSeconds parameter.

A serverless data-integration service for discovering, preparing, moving, and integrating data for analytics, machine learning, and application development. Includes a Data Catalog, crawlers, ETL jobs (Python or Scala), Glue Studio visual editor, DataBrew, and Schema Registry.
A persistent, fully managed metadata store inside AWS Glue. Used by Athena, EMR, Redshift Spectrum, and Lake Formation as a centralised Hive-compatible catalog of databases, tables, and partitions.

A data-management service that helps catalog, discover, share, and govern data stored across AWS, on-premises, and third-party sources. Provides domain-based data marketplaces with business-friendly search and approval workflows.

An intelligent enterprise search service powered by machine learning. Indexes documents from many connectors (S3, SharePoint, Salesforce, Confluence, etc.) and answers natural-language questions with relevance ranking and read-it-back excerpts.

A time-series-forecasting service that uses ML to deliver accurate forecasts. Bring your historical and related data; Forecast trains models without ML expertise and exposes them via real-time and batch inference APIs.

A real-time personalisation and recommendation service. Trains recipes on your event data, item catalog, and user attributes to deliver tailored recommendations, similar items, personalised rankings, and email/notification segmentation.

Makes it easy to build the human-review workflows often required for machine-learning predictions. Integrates with Textract, Rekognition, and custom SageMaker models; routes low-confidence outputs to human reviewers.

Fully managed retrieval-augmented-generation (RAG) workflows for Amazon Bedrock. Connects foundation models to private data sources (S3, web crawl, Confluence, Salesforce) so models answer with current company context, with built-in vector embedding and retrieval.
The most comprehensive set of AWS cost and usage data, delivered to S3 in CSV or Parquet format. Hourly or daily granularity; integrates with Athena, Redshift, and QuickSight for analysis.

A customisable billing service that supports showback and chargeback workflows for AWS solution providers and enterprises. Lets you create custom billing groups, apply pricing rules and discounts, and produce per-group invoices.
Uses machine learning to monitor AWS spend and detect anomalies. Sends alerts (email, SNS) when an unusual spend pattern is identified so cost spikes can be investigated quickly. Free to use.

An AWS Support plan between Business and Enterprise. Provides a pool of Technical Account Managers (TAMs), 30-minute response on business-critical issues, and access to most Enterprise Support benefits at a lower entry price.
A free repository of how-to articles and answers to common AWS questions, curated by AWS Support. Searchable from any AWS account; no support-plan upgrade required.

A global team of AWS experts who provide paid engagements to help customers accelerate adoption: assessments, prescriptive guidance, hands-on engineering, and architecture/security/migration projects.
Makes it easy to find, subscribe to, and use third-party data in the cloud. Subscriptions appear directly in your S3 bucket / Redshift cluster / API destination. Charges roll up onto the AWS bill.
Umbrella for the AWS cost-related services: Cost Explorer, Budgets, Cost and Usage Report, Cost Anomaly Detection, Savings Plans, and the AWS Pricing Calculator. Accessible from the Billing console.

A program offering customers free access to AWS services in three forms: Always Free (e.g. 1M Lambda requests/month), 12-Month Free (e.g. 750 hours of t2.micro), and Trials (limited-time after activation, e.g. SageMaker Studio Lab).

Migrates databases to AWS quickly and securely. Source database remains fully operational during the migration; supports homogeneous (e.g. Oracle to Oracle) and heterogeneous migrations (e.g. Oracle to Aurora MySQL) and continuous replication for change-data-capture.
Converts a source database schema and a majority of the custom code (stored procedures, views, etc.) to a format compatible with the target AWS database. Pairs with DMS to handle the data movement.

Lift-and-shift solution for rehosting any on-premises or other-cloud server onto EC2. Continuously replicates source server disks to a Staging Area Subnet, then performs cut-over with minimal downtime.

A managed cloud service that lets connected devices easily and securely interact with cloud applications and other devices. Supports MQTT, HTTP, and WebSockets; provides device registry, device shadows, and rules-engine routing to other AWS services.

Open-source edge runtime and cloud service for building, deploying, and managing intelligent device software. Lets devices run Lambda functions and ML inference locally, sync state, and operate while offline.

A fully managed service that automates each step required to analyse data from IoT devices. Filters, transforms, and enriches data before storing it in a time-series data store, then runs SQL queries or hosts notebooks for analysis.

A secure, fully managed content creation, storage, and collaboration service. Lets users create, edit, and share content stored in the cloud with built-in version control, comments, and per-folder permissions.
A secure, managed business email and calendar service with support for existing desktop and mobile clients. Compatible with Microsoft Outlook, Apple Mail, native Android/iOS apps, and IMAP/SMTP.

A multi-Region or multi-instance deployment in which all sites simultaneously serve live production traffic. Maximises availability and capacity but requires data-replication strategies that tolerate concurrent writes.

A deployment pattern with one active site serving traffic and one or more passive standbys ready to take over on failure. Simpler than active-active because writes only happen in one place; RTO/RPO depend on the replication mechanism.

An application that stores no client session data between requests on the compute layer. State lives in shared stores (DynamoDB, ElastiCache, S3). Stateless apps scale horizontally and tolerate instance failure with no user-visible impact.

An application that retains client session data on the compute layer between requests. Requires sticky sessions / instance affinity or session replication, complicating scale-out and failure recovery.

A load-balancer feature that binds a user's session to a specific target instance via a cookie. Useful for stateful apps; not recommended over making the app stateless and using ElastiCache or DynamoDB for session state.
Recovery Time Objective â€” the maximum acceptable elapsed time between a service interruption and restoration. Drives backup, replication, and failover strategy choices.
Recovery Point Objective â€” the maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time. Determines required backup / replication frequency.

A disaster-recovery strategy where a minimal version of the production environment runs at all times in a recovery Region (core services hot, application tier off). Lower cost than warm/active configurations; longer RTO.
A disaster-recovery strategy that maintains a scaled-down but functional copy of the production environment in another Region. Faster RTO than pilot light; higher cost.
The lowest-cost DR strategy: regularly back up data (and infrastructure-as-code definitions) and rebuild the environment from those backups on failure. Highest RTO/RPO of the four AWS DR strategies.

The highest-cost / lowest-RTO disaster recovery strategy. Multiple Regions serve production traffic at all times; on Region failure DNS / Global Accelerator routes traffic to remaining Regions with minimal disruption.

Migration strategy that moves an existing application to AWS with minimal changes. Fast, low-risk; doesn't take full advantage of cloud-native services. Maps to the 'Rehost' R in AWS's 6 R's framework.

Migration strategy where you make a few cloud optimisations during the move without changing the core architecture (e.g. moving a self-managed DB to RDS, or apps to managed Beanstalk). One of the AWS 6 R's.

Migration strategy that involves re-architecting an application to take full advantage of cloud-native services (serverless, microservices, managed databases). Highest upfront cost; greatest long-term benefit.

Decommissioning applications that are no longer needed during a cloud migration. Often surfaces 10-20% of workloads that can simply be turned off, freeing budget for the real migration. One of the AWS 6 R's.
Keeping certain applications on-premises (or in another cloud) for now, often because they don't yet justify migration. Reassessed periodically. One of the AWS 6 R's.
Moving from a self-managed (or on-prem) license to a different product, often SaaS (e.g. from on-prem CRM to Salesforce). One of the AWS 6 R's.

AWS's six migration strategies: Rehost (lift and shift), Replatform (lift, tinker, and shift), Repurchase (drop and shop), Refactor / Re-architect, Retire, and Retain. Most enterprise migrations use a mix of these per application.
One of the six Well-Architected pillars. Focuses on running and monitoring systems to deliver business value and continuously improve supporting processes and procedures.

The Well-Architected pillar focused on the ability of a workload to perform its intended function correctly and consistently. Includes recovering from disruptions, dynamically acquiring resources, and mitigating misconfiguration.

The Well-Architected pillar that addresses using IT and computing resources efficiently. Includes selecting the right resource types, monitoring performance, and making informed decisions as needs evolve.

The Well-Architected pillar focused on running systems to deliver business value at the lowest price point. Includes financial-management practices, expenditure awareness, and matching supply with demand.

The Well-Architected pillar that addresses protecting information, systems, and assets while delivering business value. Includes identity and access management, detection, infrastructure protection, data protection, and incident response.

The newest Well-Architected pillar (added 2021). Focuses on minimising the environmental impact of running cloud workloads through right-sizing, efficient architectures, regional choice, and managed services.
An upfront expenditure for physical assets (servers, data centres, networking gear) that depreciates over time. The traditional model for on-premises IT, replaced by OpEx in cloud computing.
An ongoing operating cost (pay-as-you-go cloud spend, electricity, support). Cloud's OpEx model lets organisations replace upfront capital investments with variable costs tied to actual usage.

The complete cost of acquiring and operating a system over its useful life, including hardware, software, energy, networking, real estate, IT labour, and security. Used to compare cloud vs on-premises economics.
The ability of a system to scale capacity up or down automatically in response to changes in demand. Implemented in AWS via Auto Scaling groups, DynamoDB on-demand, Aurora Serverless, etc.

The ability of a system to handle a growing amount of work, either by scaling vertically (larger instance) or horizontally (more instances). AWS strongly favours horizontal scaling for cloud-native workloads.

Designing a workload to continue operating despite failures of underlying components. On AWS: deploy across multiple Availability Zones, use managed services with built-in failover (RDS Multi-AZ, ELB), and design for graceful degradation.

A property of a system that lets it continue operating, possibly at reduced capability, in the presence of failures. Stronger guarantee than high availability â€” designed for zero downtime during faults.

An IAM identity that you can assume to gain temporary security credentials. Unlike an IAM user, a role has no long-term credentials or password; instead, when an entity assumes a role, it receives temporary credentials for the role session. Used to delegate access to users, applications, or AWS services (e.g. EC2 instance roles, Lambda execution roles, cross-account access).

An entity created in IAM that represents a person or application that interacts with AWS. Has a name and credentials (password and/or access keys) and zero or more attached policies. AWS best practice today is to use IAM Identity Center or federated identities for human access and reserve IAM users for service accounts or break-glass cases.

A container for an IAM role that you attach to an EC2 instance at launch (or later). Provides the AWS SDK and CLI on the instance with temporary credentials via the EC2 instance metadata service, removing the need to embed long-term access keys.

An IAM policy that you embed directly into a single user, group, or role. The policy is deleted when the principal is deleted. Useful for one-off permissions tightly coupled to a single identity; for reusable permissions, prefer customer-managed policies.

An IAM policy that you create and manage in your AWS account. Standalone (not embedded in a single identity), versionable, and attachable to multiple users, groups, or roles. Preferred over inline policies when the same permission set is reused.

The ability to grant users authenticated by an external identity provider (corporate directory, Google, Facebook, OIDC/SAML IdP) access to AWS resources without creating an IAM user for each. AWS supports SAML 2.0 federation, web identity federation (via Cognito or AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity), and IAM Identity Center.

A user authenticated by an external identity provider who receives temporary AWS credentials via STS (AssumeRoleWithSAML, AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity, or via IAM Identity Center). Has no IAM user record.

An open-standard mechanism for exchanging authentication and authorisation data between an external identity provider (Microsoft AD FS, Okta, Ping, etc.) and AWS. After the IdP returns a SAML assertion, the client calls AssumeRoleWithSAML on STS to receive temporary AWS credentials.

Identity federation using a public web identity provider ??? Login with Amazon, Facebook, Google, or any OIDC-compatible provider ??? to obtain AWS credentials via STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity. Most mobile/web apps integrate this through Amazon Cognito identity pools rather than calling STS directly.

Granting identities in one AWS account permission to use resources in another. Implemented via an IAM role with a trust policy naming the trusted account; principals in that account call STS AssumeRole to receive temporary credentials in the trusting account.

The primary resource in AWS KMS. A logical representation of a cryptographic key plus its metadata, policy, and aliases. Three types exist: AWS managed (controlled by AWS for service-side encryption), customer managed (you control rotation, deletion, policy), and AWS owned (used internally by services like S3-SSE). Formerly called Customer Master Key (CMK).

An optional KMS feature that automatically generates new key material for a customer managed symmetric key every year (configurable from 90 to 2,560 days). KMS keeps all prior key versions so existing ciphertexts remain decryptable. Asymmetric and HMAC keys do not support automatic rotation.
Cryptography where the same key encrypts and decrypts data. Faster than asymmetric and the default for KMS-managed data encryption (AES-256 in KMS). Requires secure key distribution between parties.

Cryptography using a mathematically linked key pair ??? a public key that anyone can use to encrypt or verify and a private key kept by the owner to decrypt or sign. KMS supports asymmetric keys for sign/verify (RSA, ECC) and encrypt/decrypt (RSA only) use cases.

Encryption applied by AWS to data after it arrives at the service and before it is written to storage; decryption happens transparently on read. Most-used variants on S3 are SSE-S3 (S3-managed keys, default), SSE-KMS (KMS-managed keys with audit trail), SSE-C (customer-provided keys), and DSSE-KMS (dual-layer).

Encryption that happens on the client before data is sent to AWS. AWS only ever sees ciphertext. Implemented via the AWS Encryption SDK, the DynamoDB Encryption Client, or S3 Encryption Client; the customer owns the key-management responsibility end-to-end.

The EC2 hardware configuration combining CPU, memory, storage, and networking capacity. Identified by family + generation + size (e.g. m7i.large). Families target general-purpose (m, t), compute-optimised (c), memory-optimised (r, x), storage-optimised (i, d), accelerated (p, g, inf, trn), and burstable (t) workloads.

Physically attached, block-level temporary storage on the EC2 host machine. Very high I/O but **data is lost when the instance stops, hibernates, or terminates** (and on the underlying hardware failure). Use only for buffers, caches, scratch data, or replicated data. Not all instance types include instance store.

An HTTP endpoint at 169.254.169.254 reachable only from inside an EC2 instance. Returns metadata (instance ID, region, IAM role credentials, user-data). IMDSv2 (recommended) requires a session token obtained via PUT to mitigate SSRF attacks; IMDSv1 is unauthenticated and being deprecated.

An asymmetric RSA or ED25519 key pair used for SSH (Linux) or RDP password retrieval (Windows) on an EC2 instance. AWS keeps the public key; you download the private key once at creation. Losing the private key means you cannot connect via SSH ??? recover by detaching the volume or using EC2 Instance Connect / Session Manager.

A physical EC2 server dedicated for your use, with visibility into sockets, cores, and host ID. Helps satisfy server-bound software licences (per-socket / per-core Oracle, Windows, SQL Server) and address compliance requirements. Billed per host hour; more expensive than Dedicated Instances.

An EC2 tenancy option where instances run on hardware dedicated to a single AWS account (no instance-level visibility into the host). Costs more than default shared tenancy; less expensive and less control than a Dedicated Host.

A logical grouping of EC2 instances within an Availability Zone. Three strategies: cluster (low-latency, high-throughput, same rack), partition (spread across distinct partitions for fault isolation, used by HDFS/Cassandra), and spread (each instance on distinct hardware, up to 7 per AZ).

The list of block devices (root volume, additional EBS volumes, instance-store volumes) attached to an EC2 instance at launch. Defined in the AMI and overridable per instance launch. Determines whether instance-store volumes are exposed and whether EBS volumes are deleted on termination.

The fundamental storage unit in Amazon S3. Consists of object data (up to 5 TB), a key (the full path inside the bucket), metadata (system + user-defined), a version ID (if versioning enabled), and subresources (ACL, torrent). Buckets contain objects; folders are a console abstraction over key prefixes.

The unique identifier of an object within an S3 bucket ??? a UTF-8 string up to 1,024 bytes. The full S3 URL is s3://bucket/key. S3 treats keys as flat, but slash-delimited keys (logs/2026/01/01.json) render as folders in the console and enable prefix-based filtering, lifecycle, and replication.

A set of rules attached to an S3 bucket that automatically transitions objects between storage classes (e.g. Standard ??? Standard-IA ??? Glacier Flexible Retrieval ??? Glacier Deep Archive) or expires/deletes them after a configurable age. Filters by prefix or tag; rules also clean up incomplete multipart uploads and expired delete markers.
The default S3 storage class for frequently accessed data. 99.99% availability SLA, 99.999999999% (11 9's) durability, multi-AZ, no retrieval fee, no minimum storage duration.

Storage class for data accessed less often but requiring rapid access when needed. Lower per-GB storage cost than Standard, charged per-GB retrieval fee, 30-day minimum storage duration, multi-AZ, 99.9% availability SLA.

Storage class for infrequently accessed data that does not require multi-AZ resilience. Lives in a single AZ, ~20% cheaper than Standard-IA, 99.5% availability SLA. Use for re-creatable data (thumbnails, backups already stored elsewhere).

S3 archive storage class for rarely accessed data that still needs millisecond retrieval. ~68% cheaper than Standard, 90-day minimum storage duration, per-GB retrieval fee. Good for medical images, news archives, genomic data.
Low-cost S3 archive class with 1-5 minute Expedited, 3-5 hour Standard, or 5-12 hour Bulk retrieval tiers. 90-day minimum storage duration. Replaces the older standalone S3 Glacier service.
The cheapest S3 storage class, designed for data retained 7-10+ years (compliance, regulatory). 12-hour Standard or 48-hour Bulk retrieval; 180-day minimum storage duration.

A point-in-time, incremental backup of an EBS volume stored in S3 (managed by AWS, not visible in user buckets). The first snapshot copies all written blocks; subsequent snapshots only the changed blocks. Used to create new volumes (including in other AZs/Regions via copy), and as the basis of EBS-backed AMIs.

A point-in-time backup of an RDS DB instance, stored in S3-managed storage. Either automated (taken daily during the backup window, retained per the configured period, supports PITR) or manual (taken on demand, retained until you delete them, can be shared and copied across Regions).

A container of engine-level configuration values applied to one or more RDS DB instances. Default groups are read-only; create a custom group to tune timeouts, character sets, max_connections, etc. Some parameters require a reboot to take effect (static); others apply dynamically.

The ability to restore a database to any second within a retention window. RDS PITR uses continuous backups of WAL/binlog (up to 35 days). DynamoDB PITR is enabled per table and provides per-second restore for the last 35 days.

The primary key attribute in DynamoDB whose hash determines which partition stores the item. With a partition key alone, value must be unique across the table; combined with a sort key, only the (partition, sort) pair must be unique. Skewed partition-key distribution causes 'hot partitions' that throttle.

The second component of a DynamoDB composite primary key. Items with the same partition key are stored together and sorted by this attribute. Enables efficient range queries (BETWEEN, >, <, begins_with) within a partition.

The DynamoDB throughput unit for reads. 1 RCU = 1 strongly consistent read per second, OR 2 eventually consistent reads per second, OR 0.5 transactional reads per second, for an item up to 4 KB. Larger items consume proportionally more RCUs.
The DynamoDB throughput unit for writes. 1 WCU = 1 standard write per second for an item up to 1 KB, OR 0.5 transactional writes per second. Larger items consume proportionally more WCUs.

A consistency model where reads may return stale data shortly after a write, but all replicas converge given enough time. DynamoDB's default read mode, and S3's default model for cross-Region and certain cross-bucket reads. Half the RCU cost of a strongly consistent read in DynamoDB.

A consistency model where every read returns the latest committed write. DynamoDB supports it per-request (consumes 2?? the RCU). Amazon S3 has provided strong read-after-write consistency for all objects since December 2020.

A DynamoDB numeric attribute that you increment or decrement atomically via UpdateItem with the ADD action. The operation never blocks and never returns a conflict, but is not idempotent ??? retries may double-count. Suitable for visit counters where exact accuracy isn't required.

A range of IP addresses inside a VPC, residing in exactly one Availability Zone. Resources (EC2, RDS, Lambda-in-VPC) launch into a subnet and inherit its routing, NACL, and AZ. Subnets are classified by their route table: public (default route to an internet gateway) or private (no IGW route).

A subnet whose route table has a default route (0.0.0.0/0) pointing to an internet gateway. Instances with a public or Elastic IP launched in it are reachable from the internet. Typical home for ALBs, NAT gateways, and bastion hosts.

A subnet whose route table has no default route to an internet gateway. Instances can reach the internet only via a NAT gateway / NAT instance in a public subnet, or via VPC endpoints. Standard placement for application servers and databases.

An EC2 instance that you launch from a community NAT AMI to provide outbound internet for private-subnet resources. Predates the managed NAT gateway; lower cost at small scale but you manage HA, patching, and bandwidth. NAT gateway is the modern recommendation.

A feature that captures information about IP traffic going to/from network interfaces in a VPC. Records flow tuple, action (accept/reject), and bytes. Delivered to CloudWatch Logs, S3, or Firehose. Used for security auditing, troubleshooting connectivity, and traffic analytics.

An AWS resource that represents the customer side of a Site-to-Site VPN connection: the IP address and BGP ASN of the on-prem VPN device. Paired with a virtual private gateway or Transit Gateway to establish IPsec tunnels.
The AWS-side endpoint of a Site-to-Site VPN or Direct Connect attached to a single VPC. Provides redundant IPsec tunnel endpoints. Largely superseded by Transit Gateway for multi-VPC topologies.

An Elastic Load Balancing object that groups one or more registered targets (EC2 instances, IPs, Lambda functions, or ALBs) and defines the protocol, port, and health check used to forward traffic from a listener. The ALB / NLB routing rules direct requests to a target group.

A periodic probe used to determine whether a target is available. ELB target-group health checks issue HTTP / TCP probes to instances and remove unhealthy ones from rotation. Route 53 health checks monitor endpoints (TCP, HTTP, HTTPS) and can drive DNS failover.

The modern CloudFront feature for restricting an S3 origin so it can only be accessed via CloudFront (and not directly over the public S3 endpoint). Supports SigV4, all S3 Regions, and KMS-encrypted objects. Replaces the older Origin Access Identity (OAI).
The legacy CloudFront feature for restricting access to an S3 origin to a special CloudFront identity. Still supported for compatibility but AWS recommends migrating to Origin Access Control (OAC).

A control that limits the rate at which clients can call an API or service to prevent abuse, protect downstream resources, and ensure fair sharing. API Gateway, DynamoDB, Lambda, KMS, and most other AWS services apply throttling at account / region / resource granularity and return HTTP 429 Too Many Requests when exceeded.

An API Gateway construct that grants API keys access to specific stages and methods with per-key throttle (RPS, burst) and quota (calls per day / week / month) limits. Used by SaaS APIs to differentiate tiers (free, basic, premium).

A collection of AWS resources that you provision and manage as a single unit, defined by a CloudFormation template. Stack lifecycle (create / update / delete / rollback) is atomic; resource state and dependencies are tracked by the service. Use change sets to preview an update before applying it.

A JSON or YAML text file declaring AWS resources, parameters, outputs, conditions, mappings, and metadata. Submitted to CloudFormation to create or update a stack. Templates are idempotent declarations of desired state; CloudFormation computes the resource graph and reconciles drift.
